From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Annual postseason elimination tournament of English Football League

EFL League One play-offs

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Sport | [Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") |
| Founded | 1987 |
| No. of teams | 4 |
| Region | England Wales |
| Broadcasters | * **United Kingdom:** * [Sky Sports](/wiki/Sky_Sports "Sky Sports") * **International:** * *Varies by territory* |
| Streaming partners | * **United Kingdom:** * [Sky Go](/wiki/Sky_Go "Sky Go") * [NOW TV](/wiki/NOW_(Sky) "NOW (Sky)") * **International:** * *Varies by region* |

The **EFL League One play-offs** are a series of [play-off](/wiki/Play-offs "Play-offs") matches contested by the [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") teams finishing from third to sixth in the [EFL League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") table and are part of the [EFL playoffs](/wiki/EFL_playoffs "EFL playoffs"). As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit), the play-offs comprise two semi-finals, where the team finishing third plays the team finishing sixth, and the team finishing fourth plays the team finishing fifth, each conducted as a [two-legged tie](/wiki/Two-legged_tie "Two-legged tie"). The winners of the semi-finals progress to the final which is contested at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"). The winner of the Final is promoted to the Championship league.

For the first three years, the play-off final took place over two legs, played at both side's grounds. [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.") won the first Third Division play-off final [in 1987](/wiki/1987_Football_League_Third_Division_play-off_final "1987 Football League Third Division play-off final"), requiring a [replay](/wiki/Replay_(sports) "Replay (sports)") to defeat [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C."). From 1990, the play-off final was a one-off match, hosted at the [original Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)"), while from 2001 to 2006, the final was played at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") as Wembley was being rebuilt. Since 2007, the match has been hosted at Wembley Stadium except for the [2011 final](/wiki/2011_Football_League_One_play-off_final "2011 Football League One play-off final") which took place at [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford") to avoid a clash with the [2011 UEFA Champions League final](/wiki/2011_UEFA_Champions_League_final "2011 UEFA Champions League final").

When the third tier play-offs were first contested in 1987, they were known as the Football League Third Division play-offs. From 1993 to 2004, following the creation of the [FA Premier League](/wiki/FA_Premier_League "FA Premier League") as a breakaway from the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League"), the competition became known as the Second Division play-offs, and since 2005 has taken its current name as the League One play-offs following a rebranding of the remaining three divisions of the Football League.

## Format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Format")]

[![Play-off bracket format from 2000](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Example_of_play-off_format_from_2000_First_Division_play-offs.png/500px-Example_of_play-off_format_from_2000_First_Division_play-offs.png)](/wiki/File:Example_of_play-off_format_from_2000_First_Division_play-offs.png)

An example of the play-off format, from the [2000 Second Division play-offs](/wiki/2000_Football_League_play-offs#Second_Division "2000 Football League play-offs")

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit), the League One play-offs involve the four teams that finish directly below the automatic [promotion](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") places in [EFL League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One"), the third tier of the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"). These teams meet in a series of play-off matches to determine the final team that will be promoted to the [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship"). Based on the ranking, the team finishing in third place plays the sixth-placed team, while the team in fourth plays the fifth-placed team in the "play-off semi-finals". Two ties are played over [two legs](/wiki/Two-legged_tie "Two-legged tie"), with the higher-ranked side hosting the second leg. According to the [EFL](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League"), "this is designed to give the highest finishing team an [advantage](/wiki/Home_advantage "Home advantage")".[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

The winner of each semi-final is determined by the [aggregate score](/wiki/Aggregate_score "Aggregate score") across the two legs, with the number of goals scored in each match of the tie being added together. The team with the higher aggregate score qualifies for the final. If, at the end of the regular 90 minutes of the second leg, the aggregate score is level then the match goes into [extra time](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)") where two 15-minute halves are played. If the score remains level at the end of extra time, the tie is decided by a [penalty shootout](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)"). The [away goals rule](/wiki/Away_goals_rule "Away goals rule") does not apply in the play-off semi-finals.[[2]](#cite_note-eflexp-2)

As of 2022, VAR (virtual assistance referee) will be used in the playoff final against Wycombe Wanderers and Sunderland FC.

The clubs that win the semi-finals then meet at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"), a [neutral venue](/wiki/Glossary_of_association_football_terms#N "Glossary of association football terms"), for a one-off match referred to as the "play-off final". If required, extra time and a penalty shootout can be employed in the same manner as for the semi-finals to determine the winner. The runner-up and losing semi-finalists remain in League One while the winning side is promoted.[[2]](#cite_note-eflexp-2) The match, along with the finals of the [Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship_play-offs "EFL Championship play-offs") and [League Two play-offs](/wiki/EFL_League_Two_play-offs "EFL League Two play-offs"), usually takes place over the [long weekend](/wiki/Long_weekend "Long weekend") of the second [bank holiday](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_the_United_Kingdom "Public holidays in the United Kingdom") in May.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Background")]

Main article: [English Football League play-offs](/wiki/English_Football_League_play-offs "English Football League play-offs")

The mid-1980s saw a decline in attendances at football matches and public disenchantment with English football. A number of instances of violence and tragedy struck the game. In March 1985 at the semi-final of the [1984–85 Football League Cup](/wiki/1984%E2%80%9385_Football_League_Cup "1984–85 Football League Cup") between [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") and [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") where more than 100 people were arrested after various invasions of the [Stamford Bridge](/wiki/Stamford_Bridge_(stadium) "Stamford Bridge (stadium)") pitch and more than 40 people, including 20 policemen, were injured.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201512-4) Nine days later, [violence flared](/wiki/1985_Luton_riot "1985 Luton riot") at the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") match between [Millwall](/wiki/Millwall_F.C. "Millwall F.C.") and [Luton Town](/wiki/Luton_Town_F.C. "Luton Town F.C."): seats were used as missiles against the police and resulted in Luton Town banning away supporters.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201513-5) On 11 May, 56 people were killed and 265 injured in the [Bradford City stadium fire](/wiki/Bradford_City_stadium_fire "Bradford City stadium fire") and less than three weeks later, 39 supporters died and more than 600 were injured in the [Heysel Stadium disaster](/wiki/Heysel_Stadium_disaster "Heysel Stadium disaster") where [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C.") were playing [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") in [the European Cup final](/wiki/1985_European_Cup_Final "1985 European Cup Final").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201514–15-6)

> Initially the Play-Offs would operate for two years, but if they proved popular with spectators they could become a permanent part of the calendar.

Heathrow Agreement[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201527-7)

In an attempt to persuade fans to return to the stadia, the Football League had rejected a £19 million television deal to broadcast matches live on the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") and [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") before the [1985–86 Football League](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_Football_League "1985–86 Football League") season with League president [Jack Dunnett](/wiki/Jack_Dunnett "Jack Dunnett") suggesting that "football is prepared to have a year or two with no television".[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201519-8)In December 1985, the "Heathrow Agreement" was agreed which aimed to revitalise the financial affairs of the league. It was a ten-point plan which included a structural reorganisation of the league, reducing the top tier from 22 clubs to 20, and the introduction of [play-offs](/wiki/English_Football_League_play-offs "English Football League play-offs") to facilitate the change.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201518-9) The play-offs were introduced to the end of the [1986–87 Football League season](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_in_English_football "1986–87 in English football").[[10]](#cite_note-pye-10) They were initially introduced for two years but with the proviso that if they were successful with the general public, they would be retained permanently.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201527-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

In the first two seasons, the team one place above the relegation zone in the [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division"), along with the three clubs below the automatic promotion positions in the [Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division"), took part in the play-offs.[[10]](#cite_note-pye-10) In the inaugural play-offs, Second Division Sunderland were eliminated in the semi-finals by Third Division side [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_F.C. "Gillingham F.C.") and suffered relegation to the third tier of English football for the first time in their history.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEElligate200989-11) In [the final](/wiki/1987_Football_League_Third_Division_play-off_final "1987 Football League Third Division play-off final"), Gillingham faced Second Division [Swindon Town](/wiki/Swindon_Town_F.C. "Swindon Town F.C.") but they could not be separated over the two home-and-away legs, so the tie was settled in a [replay](/wiki/Replay_(sports) "Replay (sports)"). Played at a neutral ground, [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C.")'s stadium [Selhurst Park](/wiki/Selhurst_Park "Selhurst Park") in Croydon, Swindon Town won the game 2–0 to gain promotion to the First Division, while Gillingham remained in the Second Division.[[10]](#cite_note-pye-10)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015194-12) A replay was also required the following season when [Walsall](/wiki/Walsall_F.C. "Walsall F.C.") and [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") ended their two-legged final 3–3 on aggregate.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015196-13) A penalty shootout was used to determine which side would host the replay, which Walsall won.[[14]](#cite_note-guardrep2-14) Played at [Fellows Park](/wiki/Fellows_Park "Fellows Park") two days after the second leg took place there, the match ended 4–0 to Walsall who were promoted.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015196-13)

EFL League One play-off nomenclature

| Years | Name |
| --- | --- |
| 1987–1992 | [Football League Third Division](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") play-offs |
| 1993–2004 | [Football League Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") play-offs |
| 2005–2015 | [Football League One](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") play-offs |
| 2016–present | [EFL League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") play-offs |

The primary objective of the play-offs was achieved within the first two seasons, namely the reorganisation of the four leagues with 20 clubs in the first tier and 24 in the second to fourth tiers.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201562-15) However, the popularity of the play-offs was such that the post-season games were retained and the [play-offs](/wiki/1989_Football_League_play-offs "1989 Football League play-offs") were the first to feature four teams from the Third Division:[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster201562-15) [Port Vale](/wiki/Port_Vale_F.C. "Port Vale F.C.") defeated [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") over two legs in the [1989 Football League Third Division play-off final](/wiki/1989_Football_League_Third_Division_play-off_final "1989 Football League Third Division play-off final").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015198-16) From 1990, the format of the final changed to a single match played at a neutral venue, initially the [original Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)"). The first winners of the inaugural one-off [final](/wiki/1990_Football_League_Third_Division_play-off_final "1990 Football League Third Division play-off final") were [Notts County](/wiki/Notts_County_F.C. "Notts County F.C.") who beat [Tranmere Rovers](/wiki/Tranmere_Rovers_F.C. "Tranmere Rovers F.C.") 2–0 in front of 29,252 spectators.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015200-17)

Wembley Stadium was demolished early in the 21st century to make way for a brand new stadium. The [2000 Second Division final](/wiki/2000_Football_League_Second_Division_play-off_final "2000 Football League Second Division play-off final") was the last third-tier final to be hosted beneath the *Twin Towers*. For the next six years, the finals were hosted at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in Cardiff, where Walsall needed extra time to beat [Reading](/wiki/Reading_F.C. "Reading F.C.") 3–2 in the final watched by a crowd of 50,496.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015211-18) The play-off finals returned to the new [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in 2007. The first [such final](/wiki/2007_Football_League_One_play-off_final "2007 Football League One play-off final") saw [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool_F.C. "Blackpool F.C.") defeat [Yeovil Town](/wiki/Yeovil_Town_F.C. "Yeovil Town F.C.") 2–0.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015217-19)

The [League One](/wiki/2011_Football_League_One_play-off_final "2011 Football League One play-off final") play-off final was relocated to [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford") for a single season as a result of a scheduling clash with the [2011 UEFA Champions League Final](/wiki/2011_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2011 UEFA Champions League Final").[[20]](#cite_note-20) The [2020 final](/wiki/2020_EFL_League_One_play-off_final "2020 EFL League One play-off final") was played [behind closed doors](/wiki/Behind_closed_doors_(sport) "Behind closed doors (sport)") as a result of the [COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom"). [Wycombe Wanderers](/wiki/Wycombe_Wanderers_F.C. "Wycombe Wanderers F.C.") beat [Oxford United](/wiki/Oxford_United_F.C. "Oxford United F.C.") to reach the second-tier for the first time in their history. The official attendance was 0.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Since the first play-off final, the third tier of English football's league itself has undergone a number of re-brands. In 1993, the Premier League was [formed](/wiki/Foundation_of_the_Premier_League "Foundation of the Premier League"),[[22]](#cite_note-22) a move which caused the third-tier league to be renamed as the Second Division.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 2004, the Second Division was re-branded as Football League One,[[24]](#cite_note-24) before the League's adoption of English Football League (EFL) led to a 2016 renaming as the EFL League One.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Prize")]

The financial value of winning the EFL League One play-off is derived from the additional remuneration clubs receive in the Championship. As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit) clubs in the third tier receive around £1.4 million, comprising a "basic award" and a "solidarity" payment, the latter of which is funded by the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League").[[26]](#cite_note-26) In the second tier, the total funding rises to a total of around £7 million, a fivefold increase in revenue.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The winners of the final receive a trophy.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Records")]

Nine clubs have secured promotion from the third tier of English football through the play-off final twice, Walsall becoming the first to do so in 2001 and most recently [Charlton Athletic](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") who beat [Leyton Orient](/wiki/Leyton_Orient_F.C. "Leyton Orient F.C.") in the [2025 final](/wiki/2025_EFL_League_One_play-off_final "2025 EFL League One play-off final"). Brentford have failed to be promoted via the play-offs on seven occasions. Along with Bristol City, they have lost in the final three times.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015194–225-68)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_League_One_play-offs&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-40)** Manchester City won the 1999 Football League Second Division play-off final with a 3–1 penalty shoot-out victory over Gillingham.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015209-39)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-47)** Barnsley won the 2006 Football League One play-off final with a 4–3 penalty shoot-out victory over Swansea City.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015216-46)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-53)** Huddersfield won the 2012 Football League One play-off final with an 8–7 penalty shoot-out victory over Sheffield United.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015222-52)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-56)** Rotherham United won the 2014 Football League One play-off final with a 4–3 penalty shoot-out victory over Leyton Orient.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoster2015224-55)
